section.title: Instructions on Prayer and Worship
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from doctrine to the gathered church at prayer. First of all, prayer is to be made for everyone, including rulers, grounded in the truth that there is one God and one mediator who gave Himself a ransom for all. He then directs the conduct of men and women in worship, ending with the much debated instruction about women, teaching, and authority.
section.thesis: Because God desires all kinds of people to be saved through the one mediator, Christ Jesus, the church at worship is to pray for everyone, and men and women alike are to conduct themselves in godliness, reverence, and good works.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to pray for all people grounded in the one God and one mediator; the conduct of men and women in worship; and the debated instruction on a woman learning quietly, named fairly with its strongest orthodox readings.

verses.text: First of all, then, I urge that petitions, prayers, {{enteuxis|intercessions}}, and thanksgiving be offered for everyone,
for kings and all those in authority, so that we may lead tranquil and quiet lives in all {{eusebeia|godliness}} and dignity.
This is good and pleasing in the sight of God our Savior,
who wants everyone to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth.
For there is one God, and there is one {{mesites|mediator}} between God and men, the man Christ Jesus,
who gave Himself as a {{antilytron|ransom}} for all, the testimony that was given at just the right time.
For this reason I was appointed as a preacher, an apostle, and a faithful and true teacher of the Gentiles. I am telling the truth; I am not lying about anything.
Therefore I want the men everywhere to pray, lifting up holy hands, without anger or dissension.
Likewise, I want the women to adorn themselves with respectable apparel, with modesty, and with self-control, not with braided hair or gold or pearls or expensive clothes,
but with good deeds, as is proper for women who profess to worship God.

units.label: A woman learning, and the debate

verses.text: A woman must learn in {{hesychia|quietness}} and full submissiveness.
I do not permit a woman to teach or to {{authenteo|exercise authority}} over a man; she is to remain quiet.
For Adam was formed first, and then Eve.
And it was not Adam who was deceived, but the woman who was deceived and fell into transgression.
Women, however, will be saved through childbearing, if they continue in faith, love, and holiness, with {{sophrosyne|self-control}}.

enteuxis.gloss: Intercession, a petition or approach made on behalf of another, even an appeal to a king. It is one of four words Paul heaps up for prayer, stressing that the church is to bring the needs of all people, especially outsiders and rulers, before God.

eusebeia.gloss: Godliness, true reverence for God worked out in daily life. It is a key word of the Pastoral Epistles, describing a life that honors God. Peaceful public order is desired so believers may live out this godliness undisturbed.

mesites.gloss: A mediator, one who stands between two parties to bring them together. Paul insists there is only one between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. He alone bridges the gap, being truly God and truly man, which is why prayer and salvation come through Him alone.

antilytron.gloss: A ransom, the price paid to release a captive, with the prefix anti stressing a substitute given in exchange. Christ gave Himself as this ransom for all, His life laid down in place of sinners to set them free.

hesychia.gloss: Quietness, stillness, a settled and peaceable spirit rather than mere silence. The same word describes the tranquil life all believers are to seek in verse 2. A woman is to learn in this quietness, that is, in a teachable, unclamorous manner.

authenteo.gloss: To exercise authority over, a rare word appearing only here in the New Testament. Its precise nuance is debated, ranging from a neutral holding of authority to a domineering or self-assumed control. Its rarity is one reason this passage is discussed so carefully.

sophrosyne.gloss: Self-control, soundness of mind, sober good sense. It frames the whole instruction to women, appearing in verse 9 and again in verse 15. This inner self-mastery, not outward display, marks the woman who professes to worship God.

basic.context: Paul says the first priority in worship is prayer, offered for everyone, including kings and rulers, so the church can live peaceful, godly lives. This pleases God, who wants all kinds of people saved. The reason is the gospel: one God, one mediator, Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all. Paul then directs how men and women should conduct themselves in worship, ending with a debated instruction about women learning quietly.
basic.hermeneutics: The call to prayer and the gospel ground beneath it are plainly for all time. The instruction on women, teaching, and authority is read differently by faithful Christians; the study names the main orthodox readings without pretending the debate does not exist. Whatever one's view, the passage upholds the full worth of women and their call to learn.
basic.originalLanguages: Prayer includes enteuxis, intercession, so believers may live in eusebeia, godliness. There is one mesites, mediator, Christ, who gave Himself an antilytron, ransom. A woman is to learn in hesychia, quietness, and the debated word authenteo, exercise authority, appears only here, framed by sophrosyne, self-control.
basic.typology: The one mediator fulfills the whole priestly system: where priests once stood between God and Israel, Christ is the final and perfect mediator. His ransom fulfills every offering that pointed to a life given in place of sinners.
basic.christ: The center is verses 5 and 6: one God, one mediator, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all. Salvation, prayer, and access to God all run through Him alone. He is both the ground of our praying and the content of the truth all are to come to know.
basic.god: God is our Savior who wants everyone to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth. He is one, and He is pleased when His people pray for all, including those in authority. His saving desire reaches beyond Israel to the nations, which is why Paul was made a teacher of the Gentiles.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 10 says the Son of Man came to give His life a ransom for many. Hebrews 9 presents Christ as the mediator of a new covenant. Genesis 2 and 3 stand behind Paul's appeal to creation and the fall.
basic.oneStory: God has always meant to gather a people from every nation to Himself. Here the church prays for all people because the one mediator gave Himself a ransom for all, and worship is ordered so that the household of God displays godliness. It is part of God's long work of reconciling the world to Himself through His Son.

context.literary: After the personal testimony of chapter 1, Paul turns to the ordering of public worship, beginning with the phrase first of all. The gospel core in verses 5 and 6 anchors the call to prayer, and Paul's own commission in verse 7 bridges to instructions for men and then women, moving from prayer to adornment to the debated matter of teaching.
context.historical: Ephesus was home to the cult of Artemis, with prominent female religious leadership and, it seems, false teaching that had drawn in some women, seen in 5:13 and 2 Timothy 3:6. Praying for kings mattered under Roman rule where Christians were a suspect minority. Braided hair, gold, and pearls signaled wealth and status in that culture.
context.canonical: With 1 Corinthians 11 and 14, this is one of the key New Testament passages on men and women in worship, and verses 5 and 6 are among the clearest statements of Christ as the sole mediator, treasured across the church's confessions.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To order the church's worship around prayer for all people, grounded in the one mediator, and to direct the conduct of men and women so that the gathered church reflects godliness, reverence, and sound teaching.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The call to pray for everyone and the confession of one mediator are prescriptive and universal. The instructions on adornment target the display of wealth and immodesty, so the principle, modesty and good works over ostentation, endures while the specific items are examples. The instruction on women is where readers most differ on how far its form binds every culture and how far it addresses Ephesus.

debates.question: How binding is the instruction that a woman not teach or exercise authority over a man?

views.label: A permanent creational order
views.case: Paul grounds it in creation, Adam formed first, verse 13, not merely in local custom, so many hold it establishes an abiding pattern that the office of teaching and governing authority in the church is reserved to qualified men, while women share fully in every other ministry.

views.label: A response to a specific Ephesian problem
views.case: Others, also orthodox, argue Paul addresses untaught or deceived women caught up in the false teaching, note verse 14 and 5:13; the rare word authenteo may mean domineering, so the command to learn quietly first corrects a particular disorder rather than barring all women from teaching for all time.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor Scripture's authority and the full dignity and gifting of women; they differ on scope. Each must reckon with the creation appeal in verse 13 and with the wider New Testament witness to women in ministry.

debates.question: What does saved through childbearing in verse 15 mean?

views.label: Preserved and sanctified in the ordinary calling of family life
views.case: Salvation is not earned by bearing children; rather the godly woman is kept and grows in holiness as she walks by faith, including in the sphere of motherhood, over against the false teachers who forbade marriage, 4:3.

views.label: Saved through the childbearing, the birth of Christ
views.case: Some read the definite article as pointing to the childbearing, the coming of the Messiah through a woman, so women, like all, are saved through the Seed born of Eve's line, echoing Genesis 3:15.

debates.bearing: Both guard against any works-salvation and hold that women are saved by grace through faith; the verse then either dignifies the ordinary calling or points to Christ, the promised Seed.

study.originalLanguages: Paul piles up four prayer words, including enteuxis, intercession, so the church may live in eusebeia, godliness, undisturbed. The gospel ground is stark: one God and one mesites, mediator, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself an antilytron, a substitute-ransom, for all. In worship a woman is to learn in hesychia, a quiet, teachable stillness, the same word used of the peaceable life in verse 2. The disputed term authenteo, to exercise authority, occurs only here in the New Testament, and its precise force is part of why the passage is weighed so carefully. The whole instruction is bracketed by sophrosyne, self-control or sound-mindedness, in verses 9 and 15, locating a woman's true adornment in godly character rather than display.

typology.type: The one mediator between God and men
typology.fulfillment: Christ fulfills the priesthood that once stood between God and Israel, the final Mediator of a better covenant, Hebrews 9:15.
Christ's life laid down in place of sinners fulfills every substitutionary offering, Mark 10:45.

study.christ: Verses 5 and 6 are the theological heart: there is one God and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all. His true humanity qualifies Him to stand between God and men, and His self-giving death is the price that sets captives free. All prayer rises to God through Him, all salvation comes through Him, and the truth to which God calls everyone is the truth about Him. Even the promise of verse 15, on one reading, points to Christ born of a woman.
study.god: God is called our Savior and is said to want everyone to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth. He is one, the sole God, and He is pleased when His people intercede for all, including rulers and enemies. His saving purpose reaches the nations, which is why the gospel is preached to Gentiles. He orders the worship of His household so that it displays reverence and godliness before a watching world.

crossReferences.note: The Son of Man came to give His life as a ransom for many, the saying behind the ransom for all.
Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, the fulfillment of the one mediator between God and men.
Adam formed first and Eve deceived, the creation and fall narrative Paul appeals to in verses 13 and 14.
A parallel apostolic instruction on women and speaking in the gathered assembly, read alongside this passage.

study.oneStory: God has always purposed to gather a people from every nation to worship Him. Here the church prays for all people because the one mediator gave Himself a ransom for all, and worship is ordered so the household of God shines with godliness. Prayer for kings, the confession of the one Savior, and reverent conduct all belong to God's larger work of reconciling the world to Himself through the man Christ Jesus.

quiz.q: What does Paul say should be the first priority in the church's worship?

options.text: Prayer, including intercession and thanksgiving, offered for everyone.
options.note: Verses 1 and 2 urge prayer for all people, including rulers.

options.text: Collecting an offering before anything else.
options.note: Giving matters elsewhere, but Paul names prayer for all as first of all here.

options.text: Debating the false teachers publicly.
options.note: The priority is prayer, not public dispute.

options.text: Praying only for fellow believers.
options.note: Paul explicitly widens prayer to everyone, including kings.

quiz.q: On what gospel truth does Paul ground the call to pray for all?

options.text: There is one God and one mediator, Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all.
options.note: Verses 5 and 6 supply the reason for praying for everyone.

options.text: That every religion leads to the same God.
options.note: Paul insists on one mediator, not many roads; this misreads him.

options.text: That prayer changes God's unchanging plans by force.
options.note: The ground is the one mediator, not leverage over God.

options.text: That God is indifferent to who is saved.
options.note: God wants everyone to be saved and to know the truth.

quiz.q: What is a woman's true adornment, according to Paul?

options.text: Modesty, self-control, and good deeds fitting for those who worship God.
options.note: Verses 9 and 10 locate adornment in character and good works.

options.text: The most expensive clothing and jewelry available.
options.note: Paul contrasts good deeds with gold, pearls, and costly attire.

options.text: Complete withdrawal from all public life.
options.note: The point is godly conduct in worship, not withdrawal.

options.text: Silence born of having nothing to offer.
options.note: Women profess to worship God and are called to good works, not treated as worthless.

quiz.q: How does the study fairly present the debate over verses 11 and 12?

options.text: As a genuine disagreement among orthodox Christians over the scope of the instruction.
options.note: The study names both a creational-order reading and a situational reading, each held by faithful believers.

options.text: As proof that the Bible devalues women.
options.note: Both readings uphold the full dignity and gifting of women; this conclusion misreads the text.

options.text: As a settled matter with no legitimate discussion.
options.note: The passage is genuinely debated among those who honor Scripture.

options.text: As a passage Christians should simply ignore.
options.note: The task is to weigh it carefully, not to ignore it.

quiz.q: What grounds does Paul give in verses 13 and 14 for his instruction?

options.text: The creation order and the account of the fall in Genesis.
options.note: Adam formed first, and Eve deceived, are drawn from Genesis.

options.text: The customs of Roman law courts.
options.note: Paul appeals to Genesis, not to Roman civil custom.

options.text: A vote taken by the Ephesian elders.
options.note: His reasoning is from Scripture, not a church vote.

options.text: His personal dislike of women teachers.
options.note: He grounds it in creation and the fall, not personal preference.

questions: Paul makes prayer for everyone, including rulers we may dislike, the first priority. Who in authority do you find hardest to pray for, and will you begin to?
There is one mediator between God and men. How does resting on Christ alone free you from trying to reach God on your own terms?
Christ gave Himself a ransom for all. What captivity in your life do you most need to know He has paid to release you from?
Paul locates true adornment in good deeds and self-control rather than display. Where are you tempted to invest in outward image over inward character?
Faithful Christians read the instruction on women differently. How can you hold your own conviction on this with both firmness and humility toward those who differ?

translationNotes.note: Where the BSB prints an em dash after for everyone, house style renders it as a comma leading into verse 2, for kings and all those in authority.
The em dash before the testimony is rendered as a comma per house style: a ransom for all, the testimony that was given at just the right time.
The BSB footnote notes that woman may be rendered wife, and again in verse 12, one reason the passage is debated.
Exercise authority renders authenteo, a rare word found only here in the New Testament; the footnote also offers over her husband for over a man.
The BSB footnote gives the literal singular, She, however, will be saved, and childbearing carries the definite article, which some link to the birth of Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 2:1-15 this session. Em dashes in verses 1, 2, and 6 have been rendered as commas per house style.
